mnemonics.title: PEMDAS for Logic Connectives
mnemonics.content: Order of precedence: NOT → AND → OR → IMPLIES → IFF. Remember: 'Not Andy Ors Impressive Friends' - N(¬) > A(∧) > O(∨) > I(→) > F(↔)

mnemonics.title: Modus Ponens Chant
mnemonics.content: If P then Q. P is true, therefore Q is true. Song: 'If P implies Q, and P is the way, then Q must follow, come what may!'

mnemonics.title: Modus Tollens Melody
mnemonics.content: If P then Q. Q is false, therefore P is false. Tune: 'Not Q, not Q, if P then Q, so not P is true!'

mnemonics.title: Quantifier Swap
mnemonics.content: Universal then Existential: 'For All, There Exists' - FATEE. ∀x∃y means 'For every x, there exists some y'

mnemonics.title: De Morgan's Laws
mnemonics.content: NOT (A AND B) = NOT A OR NOT B. 'Break the line, change the sign' - negate the whole, flip the connective

mnemonics.title: Truth Table Rows
mnemonics.content: For n propositions: 2^n rows. 'Binary count from 0 to 2^n minus 1' - count in binary for complete coverage

acronyms.usage: A statement that is either true or false
Basic proposition that cannot be broken down further
Formula as AND of ORs - useful for SAT solvers
Formula as OR of ANDs - useful for pattern matching
Collection of propositions representing knowledge in AI
The statement we want to entail from the knowledge base
States that a property holds for all elements in domain
States that at least one element in domain satisfies property
Knowledge base logically implies the query
Logic with quantifiers, predicates, constants and functions

visualTips.visualization: Draw a 2^n row grid. First half all True for first proposition, then alternate every 1 row, then every 2, then every 4 - like binary counting from 0 to 2^n-1
Think of circles - AND as the intersection (overlap only), OR as the union (both circles combined). Shade the Venn diagram regions
Draw a one-way street arrow. If P is TRUE and leads to Q, then Q must be TRUE. If P is FALSE, the street is closed - anything can happen (TRUE)
Imagine a giant container with ALL elements inside. Draw a box containing every element, all stamped with the same property
Think of a magnifying glass finding at least ONE element with the property. Draw one element highlighted with a sparkle
P → Q and P form a domino chain. P falls (true), so Q must fall (true) - visual domino effect
Bound variables are 'caged' inside quantifiers (draw a box around them with ∀ or ∃). Free variables roam freely outside boxes

analogies.analogy: Like a light switch - it's either ON (True) or OFF (False), no in-between
Like a series circuit - all bulbs must be working (True) for current to flow. One broken bulb breaks the circuit
Like a parallel circuit - current flows if at least one path is available
Like a mirror - flips True to False and vice versa. The reflection is the opposite of reality
Like a promise or contract: 'If you finish your homework (P), then you can play (Q).' If P is violated, the promise is kept vacuously
Like a question with parameters: 'IsXGreaterThan(Y)' - a function that returns True/False based on inputs
Universal (∀) is like a strict teacher checking EVERY student's homework. Existential (∃) is like a detective finding ONE suspect
Like a math proof: if KB are your axioms and steps, α is what you can prove. KB ⊨ α means α is guaranteed by KB
Like specific names: 'John', 'Paris' - constants with no variables inside, fixed values
Like a recipe - given ingredients (premises), you follow rules (inference rules) to produce new dishes (conclusions)
